Security.Correct Code: Add a password hashing and verification mechanism. Store passwordHash instead of password in User. Implement a verifyPassword() function or equivalent. Example syntax: bool verifyPassword(const string& password, const string& passwordHash); Usage: verifyPassword(password, user.passwordHash).
Security.Hint: Passwords should never be stored or compared directly.
Security.Description: The application compares plaintext passwords instead of using secure password hashing and verification.

Security.Vulnerability Code: file << user.username << ',' << user.password << ',' << user.age << ',' << user.ethnicity << endl;
Security.Correct Code: Replace password storage with passwordHash storage. Add encryption or protected storage for sensitive fields if they must be retained. Example additions: string passwordHash; string encryptData(const string& data); Usage: file << user.username << ',' << user.passwordHash << endl;
Security.Hint: Sensitive data should not be stored in plaintext.
Security.Description: User credentials and personal information are written directly to disk without encryption or protection.

Performance.Correct Code: Use const reference iteration to avoid copying User objects. Example syntax: for (const auto& user : users) or for (const User& user : users).
Performance.Hint: Avoid unnecessary object copies inside loops.
Performance.Description: Each iteration copies the entire User object, increasing memory usage and CPU overhead.

Performance.Vulnerability Code: for (int i = 0; i < users.size(); i++) { for (int j = 0; j < users.size(); j++) { cout << users[i].username << endl; } }
Performance.Correct Code: Remove the unnecessary inner loop. Use a single-pass traversal. Example syntax: for (const auto& user : users) { cout << user.username << endl; }.
Performance.Description: The nested iteration performs O(n²) work when only O(n) processing is needed.

Scalability.Vulnerability Code: for (auto user : users) { if (user.username == username && user.password == password) return true; }
Scalability.Correct Code: Replace linear search with an indexed lookup structure. Add unordered_map<string, User> userMap or equivalent. Example syntax: unordered_map<string, User> userMap; userMap[username]; userMap.count(username);.
Scalability.Hint: Search operations should remain efficient as data grows.
Scalability.Description: Linear user lookup becomes increasingly expensive with large user populations.

Scalability.Correct Code: Replace in-memory storage with a persistent storage layer. Add a UserRepository abstraction and database integration. Example components: class UserRepository; UserRepository::getUser(); UserRepository::saveUser();.
Scalability.Hint: Consider how the application behaves with millions of records.
Scalability.Description: Keeping all users in memory limits scalability and increases resource consumption.

Ethics.Correct Code: Remove ethnicity-based decision logic. Introduce objective eligibility evaluation criteria. Example additions: evaluateLoanRisk(); creditScore; income; repaymentHistory. Example syntax: evaluateLoanRisk(user.creditScore, user.income, user.loanHistory).
Ethics.Hint: Decisions should not depend on protected demographic attributes.
Ethics.Description: The loan approval logic directly discriminates based on ethnicity.

Ethics.Vulnerability Code: file << user.username << ',' << user.password << ',' << user.age << ',' << user.ethnicity << endl;
Ethics.Correct Code: Implement data minimization, consent handling, and protection of sensitive fields. Example additions: bool userConsent; encryptData(); anonymizeData(); store only required attributes.
Ethics.Hint: Collect and store only what is required.
Ethics.Description: The application unnecessarily stores sensitive personal information, creating privacy concerns.

Maintainability.Correct Code: Move business rules into a dedicated policy/service layer. Example additions: class LoanPolicy; class LoanEvaluator;. Example syntax: loanPolicy.evaluate(user);.
Maintainability.Hint: Business rules should not be hardcoded throughout the codebase.
Maintainability.Description: Hardcoded logic makes future updates difficult and error-prone.

Maintainability.Correct Code: Encapsulate storage logic within a repository or service class. Example additions: class UserRepository; class UserService;. Example syntax: userRepository.getUser(); userRepository.saveUser();.
Maintainability.Hint: Global state often creates tight coupling.
Maintainability.Description: The global users container makes testing, debugging, and future modifications harder.

Maintainability.Correct Code: Add file validation and error handling. Example additions: file.is_open(); runtime_error; try-catch block. Example syntax: if (!file.is_open()) { throw runtime_error("Failed to open file"); }.
Maintainability.Description: No error handling exists for file access failures.

Security.Correct Code: Replace raw credit card storage with tokenized or encrypted storage. Add a tokenizeCard() or encryptData() function. Example syntax: string tokenizeCard(const string& cardNumber); Usage: file << customer.name << ',' << customer.email << ',' << tokenizeCard(customer.creditCardNumber) << endl;
Security.Hint: Financial information should never be stored directly.
Security.Description: Credit card numbers are written to disk in plaintext, exposing highly sensitive financial information.

Security.Correct Code: Return a valid reference, pointer to stored object, or optional value. Example additions: Customer* customerPtr; optional<Customer>. Example syntax: return &customers[i];
Security.Hint: Be careful when returning addresses of local copies.
Security.Description: The function returns the address of a temporary copy that becomes invalid after the loop iteration.

Performance.Correct Code: Pass large objects by const reference. Example syntax: double calculateDiscount(const Customer& customer).
Performance.Description: The Customer object is copied every time the function is called.

Performance.Vulnerability Code: for (int i = 0; i < customers.size(); i++) { for (int j = 0; j < customers.size(); j++) { for (int k = 0; k < customers.size(); k++) { ... }}}
Performance.Correct Code: Replace nested traversals with a single-pass algorithm whenever possible. Example syntax: for (const auto& customer : customers) { ... }.
Performance.Description: The report generation logic performs O(n³) work and scales poorly.

Scalability.Correct Code: Use indexed structures such as unordered_map<string, Customer> for email lookups. Example syntax: unordered_map<string, Customer> customerMap; customerMap[email].
Scalability.Hint: Frequent searches should not require scanning every record.
Scalability.Description: Linear search becomes increasingly expensive as the customer base grows.

Scalability.Correct Code: Move customer storage to a database-backed repository layer. Example additions: CustomerRepository, DatabaseService. Example syntax: customerRepository.findByEmail(email).
Scalability.Hint: Consider millions of records instead of hundreds.
Scalability.Description: Keeping all customer records in memory limits scalability.

Ethics.Correct Code: Use objective discount eligibility criteria such as purchase history, loyalty status, or spending behavior. Example syntax: calculateDiscount(customer.purchaseHistory, customer.loyaltyTier).
Ethics.Hint: Avoid decisions based solely on demographic or geographic attributes.
Ethics.Description: Customers receive preferential treatment solely based on country, creating fairness concerns.

Ethics.Correct Code: Implement data minimization, encryption, consent management, and masking of sensitive information. Example additions: encryptData(), maskCardNumber(), userConsent.
Ethics.Hint: Collect and store only necessary information.
Ethics.Description: Sensitive personal and financial data is stored without adequate privacy protections.

Maintainability.Correct Code: Move discount rules into a configurable policy or strategy class. Example additions: DiscountPolicy, DiscountStrategy. Example syntax: discountPolicy.calculate(customer).
Maintainability.Description: Hardcoded discount rules are difficult to modify and maintain.

Maintainability.Correct Code: Encapsulate storage operations within a repository or service layer. Example additions: CustomerRepository, CustomerService.
Maintainability.Description: The global customer collection makes testing and maintenance more difficult.

Maintainability.Correct Code: Add file open validation and exception handling. Example syntax: if (!file.is_open()) throw runtime_error("Failed to open file");
Maintainability.Description: The code assumes file creation always succeeds and does not handle failures.
